checks.a_analysis: The output faithfully represents the text found in the source PDF. The headings, bullet points, and body text match the content visible on the slides. The detailed text in the 'Crisis Communications' section (Page 17) is correctly transcribed from the SharePoint site screenshot image embedded on that slide, and thus does not constitute fabrication.

All URLs (e.g., bit.ly/2BgpYYo, go.usa.gov/xdhkd) and email addresses (e.g., globalsecurity@contoso.electronics.com) present in the output were verified against the screenshots of the SharePoint sites included in the source PDF (specifically Page 17). No fabricated contact information was found.

While the provided output snippet omits 15 sections for brevity, the structure indicates that the original conversion process accounted for all 21 pages of the source document. No pages appear to have been silently dropped in the conversion process represented by the headings.

The table 'Response teams' on Page 17 contains all four entries (Global Security, Global HR, AskHR, Crisis response) shown in the source image, organized into a grid that correctly reflects the information.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: There are significant text omissions in the 'Overview' (Page 2) and 'Why use SharePoint Online' (Page 3) sections. In both cases, the output includes the list headers but entirely omits the descriptive paragraphs that follow them in the source. For example, in the 'Overview' section, the paragraph starting with 'Microsoft 365 SharePoint Online is a smart choice...' and the subsequent three paragraphs are missing. Similarly, on Page 3, the descriptions for 'Microsoft 365 Integration', 'Improved Security', and 'Collaboration' are missing. This constitutes a loss of more than 50% of the text for these sections.
checks.b_evidence_source: Microsoft 365 SharePoint Online is a smart choice for your organization as it can increase productivity, streamline communication, and enhance collaboration among your fellow employees. (and 3 other paragraphs in the Overview section); M365 SharePoint Online seamlessly integrates with other Microsoft 365 tools, providing a unified experience for employees and streamlining workflows. (and 2 other paragraphs in the Why use SharePoint Online section)
checks.b_evidence_output: [The list items are present but the descriptive paragraphs following them are missing in the JSON elements for these sections.]

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: On Page 17, within the 'Response teams' table, the output contains an OCR error. It transcribes 'crises' as 'crosses' in the Global Security description, which alters the meaning of the sentence.
checks.b_evidence_source: Get the latest on travel, and work from home procedures and crises.
checks.b_evidence_output: Get the latest on travel, and work from home procedures and crosses.

checks.a_analysis: Examination of the output and source document shows that no text blocks or sections have been duplicated in the conversion process. Each slide's content is represented exactly once.

The heading hierarchy is generally preserved, with slide titles correctly tagged as H1. On the complex dashboard slide (Slide 17), the hierarchy uses H2 and H3 correctly to nest sub-sections like 'Response teams' under 'WE ARE HERE TO HELP'. No heading levels are skipped.

Slide 17 contains a visual grid of four response team items. The output correctly represents this structure as a 2x2 table, grouping the appropriate text and identifiers within each cell as per the source layout.

The reading order on Slide 17 (Crisis Communications) is logically flawed. The output interleaves headers from the left and right columns of the dashboard (e.g., H2 'THE LATEST UPDATES' from the left column is followed by H2 'News from leadership' from the right column, then H2 'WE ARE HERE TO HELP' back in the left column). This zig-zagging between columns disrupts the intended flow of information. Additionally, the dashboard screenshot navigation text is placed at the top of the section, which is a minor but acceptable accessibility adjustment.
checks.b_evidence_source: Visual layout of Slide 17 shows two distinct columns: Left (Latest Updates, Help, Ask feed) and Right (News, Resources, CDC/WHO feeds).
checks.b_evidence_output: H2 THE LATEST UPDATES (reading_order 89) -> H2 News from leadership (91) -> H2 WE ARE HERE TO HELP (92) -> H2 Additional resources (95) -> H2 Ask Crisis Management (97).

checks.a_analysis: On Slides 2 (Overview) and 3 (Why use SharePoint Online), the bulleted items are converted into lists, but the conversion is incomplete. The output captures the bolded sub-headings as list items but entirely omits the following descriptive paragraphs that belong to those items. This results in truncated lists that lose the majority of the slide's textual information.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slide 2: "Why Microsoft 365 Intranet is a Smart Choice [Bullet Header] / Microsoft 365 SharePoint Online is a smart choice... [Accompanying Paragraph]"
checks.b_evidence_output: List item: "Why Microsoft 365 Intranet is a Smart Choice" (The accompanying paragraph is missing from the output).
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections 'Overview' and 'Why use SharePoint Online', Pages 2 and 3

checks.a_analysis: The URL 'https://lookbook.microsoft.com' which appears at the bottom of the Crisis Communications slide (and several others) is missing from the output.
checks.b_evidence_output: The text is absent between reading_order 104 and 106 in the Crisis Communications section.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly transforms the landscape slide deck into a single-column vertical flow, which is standard for responsive web documents. The visual organization follows the logical sequence of the slides. Information is presented in the same order as the source PDF. There are no layout changes that confuse the meaning or intent of the original document.

The source PDF does not contain any formal tables; the content consists primarily of text blocks, bulleted lists, and images. The HTML output reflects this structure accurately without introducing any misrendered table elements.

All meaningful images from the source PDF, including the AI-generated illustrations on pages 1-15, the SharePoint site screenshots on pages 16-19, and the meeting room image on page 20, are present and correctly rendered in the HTML output. There are no broken image icons or missing visual content.

There is no visual duplication of body content in the HTML output. While section headings appear in both the Table of Contents and the document body, this is an expected design feature for navigation and accessibility. The text extracted from screenshots (e.g., the 'Crisis Communications' site text in the HTML corresponding to page 17) provides the content contained within the image and does not duplicate other text on the page.
